section.title: Principles of Marriage
section.sectionRationale: Having addressed sexual immorality, Paul turns to the questions the Corinthians wrote about marriage, singleness, and sexual duty. Against both those who prized abstinence even within marriage and those who excused immorality, he teaches mutual self-giving in marriage, honors singleness and marriage each as a gift, forbids divorce, shows how a believing spouse sanctifies the home, and grounds it all in the principle of remaining faithfully in the calling in which God has placed you, because you were bought at a price.
section.thesis: Marriage is a good gift in which husband and wife belong to one another in mutual self-giving, singleness is likewise a gift, divorce is forbidden and the believing spouse sanctifies the home, and above all each believer, bought at a price, should live out their faith in the situation to which God has called them.
section.frameNote: Four movements: mutual duty and self-giving in marriage; directions to the unmarried, widows, and married against divorce; the mixed marriage and the sanctifying believer; and the governing rule, remain in your calling, for you were bought at a price.

verses.text: Now for the matters you wrote about: It is good to abstain from sexual relations.
But because there is so much {{porneia|sexual immorality}}, each man should have his own wife, and each woman her own husband.
The husband should fulfill his marital duty to his wife, and likewise the wife to her husband.
The wife does not have authority over her own body, but the husband. Likewise the husband does not have authority over his own body, but the wife.

Do not deprive each other, except by mutual consent and for a time, so you may devote yourselves to prayer. Then come together again, so that Satan will not tempt you through your lack of self-control.
I say this as a concession, not as a command.
I wish that all men were as I am. But each man has his own {{charisma|gift}} from God; one has this gift, another has that.
Now to the unmarried and widows I say this: It is good for them to remain unmarried, as I am.
But if they cannot control themselves, let them marry. For it is better to marry than to burn with passion.
To the married I give this command (not I, but the Lord): A wife must not {{chorizo|separate}} from her husband.
But if she does, she must remain unmarried or else be reconciled to her husband. And a husband must not divorce his wife.
To the rest I say this (I, not the Lord): If a brother has an unbelieving wife and she is willing to live with him, he must not divorce her.
And if a woman has an unbelieving husband and he is willing to live with her, she must not divorce him.

For the unbelieving husband is {{hagiazo|sanctified}} through his believing wife, and the unbelieving wife is sanctified through her believing husband. Otherwise your children would be unclean, but now they are holy.
But if the unbeliever leaves, let him go. The believing brother or sister is not bound in such cases. God has called you to live in peace.
How do you know, wife, whether you will save your husband? Or how do you know, husband, whether you will save your wife?
Regardless, each one should lead the life that the Lord has assigned to him and to which God has called him. This is what I prescribe in all the churches.
Was a man already circumcised when he was called? He should not become uncircumcised. Was a man still uncircumcised when called? He should not be circumcised.
Circumcision is nothing and uncircumcision is nothing. Keeping God’s commandments is what counts.
Each one should remain in the situation he was in when he was {{kaleo|called}}.
Were you a slave when you were called? Do not let it concern you; but if you can gain your freedom, take the opportunity.
For he who was a slave when he was called by the Lord is the Lord’s freedman. Conversely, he who was a free man when he was called is Christ’s slave.
You were bought at a price; do not become slaves of men.
Brothers, each one should remain in the situation he was in when God called him.

porneia.gloss: Sexual immorality of every kind. In a city saturated with it, Paul commends marriage as the God-given context for sexual life, not as a mere remedy but as the honorable and normal setting in which desire is rightly expressed within faithful covenant love.

charisma.gloss: A gift of grace, something freely given by God. Paul calls both singleness and marriage a charisma, a grace fitted to each person. Neither state is second class; each is a gift from God to be received and used for His service.

chorizo.gloss: To separate, to part, to divide. Paul relays the Lord's own command that a wife must not separate from her husband. The word underlines the permanence of the marriage bond and the seriousness of tearing apart what God has joined.

hagiazo.gloss: To sanctify, to set apart as holy. The unbelieving spouse is sanctified through the believing partner, and the children are holy. This is not automatic salvation but a covenantal setting apart: the believing spouse brings the household into the sphere of God's grace and gospel witness.

kaleo.gloss: To call, to summon. God's effective call to salvation reaches people in every station of life. Paul's repeated point is that the call does not require changing one's outward situation; the believer is to live faithfully for Christ right where the call found them.

basic.context: The Corinthians had written to Paul with questions about sex, marriage, and singleness. Some thought the holiest thing was to avoid sex altogether, even in marriage. Paul answers with balance: within marriage, husband and wife owe themselves to each other; singleness and marriage are both gifts from God; married couples must not divorce; and where one spouse believes and the other does not, the believer should stay and let the gospel work. The thread running through it all is to remain faithfully with God in the life you were called in, because you were bought at a price.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul distinguishes the Lord's direct command, do not divorce, from his own Spirit-led counsel for new situations the Gospels did not directly address, such as mixed marriages. He is not lowering the authority of his teaching but showing which words trace directly to Jesus. The section is pastoral wisdom, applying gospel principles to real questions.
basic.originalLanguages: Marriage guards against porneia, sexual immorality, by giving desire its rightful home. Both singleness and marriage are a charisma, a gift of grace from God. The Lord forbids a wife to chorizo, to separate, from her husband. A believing spouse can hagiazo, sanctify, set apart, the unbelieving partner and children. And the governing idea is kaleo, God's call, which reaches us in every station and asks us to remain faithfully there.
basic.typology: Marriage itself points beyond itself: the one flesh union of husband and wife is a shadow of Christ and His church. The sanctifying of a household through one believer echoes how holiness spreads from the covenant head to those under him, fulfilled in Christ who sanctifies His people.
basic.christ: The command against divorce is the Lord's own, showing Jesus as the authority over marriage. The deepest ground of the chapter is that we were bought at a price: Christ's purchase makes every believer, slave or free, married or single, the Lord's freedman and Christ's slave, belonging first and finally to Him.
basic.god: God is the giver of every good gift, including both marriage and singleness. He calls people to Himself in every walk of life, and He is a God of peace, not compulsion. He works even through a believing spouse to set apart a home for grace.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 2 gives the one flesh union that underlies Paul's teaching. Malachi 2 records God's hatred of divorce and His purpose for faithful marriage. Mark 10 preserves Jesus' own command that no one separate what God joined. Ephesians 5 shows marriage as a picture of Christ and the church.
basic.oneStory: From the first marriage in the garden to the marriage of the Lamb, Scripture treats the union of husband and wife as good and as a sign of God's covenant love. Paul applies that story to Corinth: honor marriage, honor singleness, keep faith with your spouse, and above all keep faith with the Lord who bought you, wherever He has called you.

context.literary: This is the first of Paul's now about replies to the Corinthians' letter, marked by direct address to distinct groups: the married, the unmarried and widows, mixed couples, and every believer. It weaves quoted questions, the Lord's command, Paul's own counsel, and a repeated refrain, remain in the calling in which you were called, which frames verses 17 to 24.
context.historical: Corinthian culture ran to two extremes, rampant immorality on one side and an ascetic contempt for the body on the other, with some claiming that spiritual people should renounce sex even in marriage. Paul steers between both errors, affirming the goodness of marital union and the honor of singleness while refusing both license and legalism.
context.canonical: Paul builds on Genesis and on Jesus' teaching about marriage and divorce, and his words stand alongside Ephesians 5 and 1 Peter 3 in the New Testament's vision of marriage. The refrain about remaining in one's calling anticipates the broader biblical truth that God meets and uses people in every ordinary station of life.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To correct both immorality and a false asceticism by teaching mutual self-giving in marriage, the goodness of both marriage and singleness, the permanence of the marriage bond, and the principle of faithful contentment in the calling God has assigned.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul explicitly labels his counsel: verse 6 is a concession, verses 10 to 11 are the Lord's command, verse 12 is his own apostolic instruction. The abiding prescriptions are mutual marital faithfulness, the prohibition of divorce, and living out one's faith in one's God-given situation; the concessions address particular pressures.

debates.question: Does Paul permit remarriage after a marriage breaks down?

views.label: Remarriage restricted
views.case: Verse 11 tells the separated wife to remain unmarried or be reconciled, suggesting the bond endures and remarriage is not freely open while the spouse lives.

views.label: The abandoned believer is not bound
views.case: Verse 15 says the believer deserted by an unbeliever is not bound, which many read as freedom from the marriage, and thus in some cases freedom to remarry in the Lord.

debates.bearing: All orthodox readings uphold the permanence of marriage and forbid casual divorce; they differ on how far verse 15 releases an abandoned believer, and pastoral care must weigh both texts with compassion and holiness.

debates.question: What does it mean that the unbelieving spouse and children are sanctified?

views.label: Covenantal setting apart, not automatic salvation
views.case: The household is brought within the sphere of grace and gospel witness through the believer, but each person still needs personal faith, as verse 16 shows by asking whether the spouse will be saved.

views.label: A ground for hope and continued marriage
views.case: The holiness of the home is Paul's reason not to divorce; the believer's presence is a means God may use toward the salvation of the family.

debates.bearing: Both readings deny that sanctified here means already saved; it assures the believer that the marriage and children are not defiled but set within reach of God's saving grace.

study.originalLanguages: Paul answers the Corinthians' questions with careful words. Marriage gives desire its honorable home over against porneia, sexual immorality. Both marriage and singleness are a charisma, a gift of grace, so neither is to be despised. The Lord commands that a wife not chorizo, separate, from her husband, marking the permanence of the bond. A believing spouse can hagiazo, set apart as holy, the unbelieving partner and the children, bringing the home within the sphere of grace. And over it all stands kaleo, God's call, which reaches people slave or free, circumcised or not, and bids each remain faithfully where the call found them.

typology.fulfillment: Points beyond itself to the union of Christ and His church, the mystery Paul unfolds in Ephesians 5.
Foreshadows how Christ, the covenant head, sanctifies all who are joined to Him, setting His people apart as holy.

study.christ: Christ is the authority behind the command not to divorce, for Paul cites the Lord's own word. And Christ is the owner of every believer: because we were bought at a price, the slave who is called becomes the Lord's freedman and the free man becomes Christ's slave. Whatever our earthly station, our deepest identity is that we belong to Jesus, who purchased us and now assigns us our place and our peace.
study.god: God is the giver of gifts, granting to one the grace of marriage and to another the grace of singleness, and He is honored in both. He is the God who calls people to Himself out of every situation and does not require them to abandon their station to serve Him. He is a God of peace, not coercion, and He works patiently, even through a believing spouse, to draw a household toward Himself.

crossReferences.note: A man leaves and is united to his wife, and they become one flesh, the union underlying Paul's teaching on marriage.
Jesus' command that what God has joined together, let no one separate, the word Paul relays in verses 10 to 11.
God's witness to the marriage covenant and His hatred of faithless divorce, background to Paul's prohibition.
Marriage as a living picture of Christ and the church, deepening the goodness Paul affirms here.
You were bought at a price, the redemption principle Paul reapplies in verse 23 to every calling.

study.oneStory: Marriage begins in the garden as a good gift and a sign, and it ends in the marriage of the Lamb to His bride. Between the two, Paul teaches the Corinthians to honor marriage and singleness alike, to keep faith with their spouses, and to live for God in whatever station they occupy. The reason is the gospel itself: Christ has bought a people at a price, so whether married or single, slave or free, they belong to Him and are called to faithful contentment in His service.

quiz.q: What does Paul teach about sexual duty within marriage?

options.text: Husband and wife each belong to the other and should not deprive one another, except briefly by mutual consent.
options.note: Verses 3 to 5 teach mutual self-giving and warn against withholding.

options.text: The husband alone has authority, and the wife must simply submit.
options.note: Paul strikingly makes the authority mutual: each has authority over the other's body.

options.text: Married couples should abstain permanently to be more spiritual.
options.note: Paul warns that prolonged deprivation opens the door to temptation.

options.text: Sexual duty may be set aside indefinitely for prayer.
options.note: Only for a time and by mutual consent; then come together again.

quiz.q: How does Paul regard marriage and singleness?

options.text: Each is a gift from God, suited to different people.
options.note: Verse 7 calls each state a charisma, a gift of grace.

options.text: Singleness is holy and marriage is a lesser compromise.
options.note: Paul honors both; he does not treat marriage as merely inferior.

options.text: Marriage is commanded for everyone.
options.note: Paul commends singleness too and wishes others could share his gift.

options.text: Both are burdens to be endured, not gifts.
options.note: Paul explicitly calls each a gift from God.

quiz.q: What is the Lord's own command to the married?

options.text: A wife must not separate from her husband, and a husband must not divorce his wife.
options.note: Verses 10 to 11 relay Jesus' command against divorce.

options.text: Couples may divorce freely if they are unhappy.
options.note: This is the opposite of the Lord's command.

options.text: A separated wife may remarry anyone at once.
options.note: Paul says she should remain unmarried or be reconciled.

options.text: Only the husband is bound; the wife is free.
options.note: The command binds both husband and wife alike.

quiz.q: What does Paul say about a marriage where only one spouse believes?

options.text: The believer should stay if the unbeliever is willing, for the home is sanctified.
options.note: Verses 12 to 14 urge the believer not to divorce a willing unbelieving spouse.

options.text: The believer must divorce the unbeliever to stay pure.
options.note: Paul says the reverse: do not divorce a willing spouse.

options.text: The believer may leave only if the unbeliever departs.
options.note: True in part; if the unbeliever leaves, let him go, but the believer should not initiate it.

options.text: The marriage is meaningless and should be ignored.
options.note: Paul takes it seriously, saying the home is set apart and the spouse may yet be saved.

quiz.q: What is the governing principle Paul repeats in verses 17 to 24?

options.text: Each believer should remain faithful to God in the situation to which he was called.
options.note: The refrain is to live out the calling in the place God's call found you.

options.text: Every believer must change their circumstances after conversion.
options.note: Paul teaches the opposite: remain in your calling.

options.text: Outward status like circumcision determines your standing with God.
options.note: Paul says circumcision is nothing; keeping God's commandments counts.

options.text: Slaves should never seek freedom under any circumstances.
options.note: Paul does say gain your freedom if you can, though the main point is faithful contentment.

quiz.q: Why can a believer serve God contentedly in any station of life?

options.text: Because they were bought at a price and belong to the Lord.
options.note: Verse 23 grounds identity in Christ's purchase, above every earthly status.

options.text: Because earthly circumstances do not matter at all to God.
options.note: They matter, but they are relativized by belonging to Christ.

options.text: Because status can be earned through hard work.
options.note: The point is redemption received, not status earned.

options.text: Because freedom is the only truly Christian condition.
options.note: Paul values freedom, but says even a slave is the Lord's freedman.

questions: Paul teaches that husband and wife belong to one another in mutual self-giving. If you are married, where might selfishness be creeping in, and what would generous love look like instead?
Both singleness and marriage are called gifts from God. How can you receive your current season, whichever it is, as a genuine gift rather than a lack?
The Lord commands married believers not to divorce. What does it look like to fight for your marriage, or to honor the permanence of marriage in how you speak of it?
A believing spouse can be a means of grace in the home. If you share a home with someone who does not believe, how might your quiet faithfulness point them to Christ?
Remain in the calling in which you were called. Are you waiting for different circumstances to serve God, when He is calling you to be faithful right where you are?

translationNotes.note: It is good to abstain from sexual relations renders a phrase that reads literally not to touch a woman, an idiom for sexual relations; many read it as a Corinthian slogan Paul takes up and carefully qualifies in the verses that follow.

Paul distinguishes the Lord's own command, do not divorce, from his own apostolic instruction to the rest; this marks which words trace directly to Jesus' earthly teaching, not a difference in authority.
Sanctified here means set apart within the sphere of grace and covenant, not automatically saved; verse 16 still asks whether the spouse will be saved, showing personal faith is needed.
Bought at a price echoes 6:20, applying the language of redemption to every station of life; because Christ owns us, no earthly status finally defines us.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 7:1-24; any em dashes rendered as commas per the no-dash style rule, with wording unchanged.
